issues.body: VALID_RECORD_PROVIDERS includes `fleet`, but `_cmd_record` does not enforce the documented provider/class pairing. Thus a catalog-controlled worker provider of `fleet` with an ordinary exit class such as `auth` passes the new guard. The subsequent `if args.exit_class == CLASS_ZERO_DISPATCH or args.provider == "fleet"` also replaces the account with the fleet sentinel and writes the record. `fleet` is documented here as the pseudo-provider for the single fleet-wide zero-dispatch signal, not a valid account provider; admitting other fleet records can corrupt health classification and violates the intended fail-closed catalog backstop. The self-test covers unknown, anthropic, and injection-shaped values but misses this boundary case.
issues.fix_hint: Enforce the invariant as a pair: accept `fleet` only when the raw exit class is `zero-dispatch`, reject `zero-dispatch` for non-fleet providers, and keep catalog-backed per-account calls restricted to `anthropic` or `openai`. Add assertions that `fleet` + ordinary class and real-provider + `zero-dispatch` both return nonzero without touching the ledger, while `fleet` + `zero-dispatch` writes exactly once.
issues.title: Provider guard permits catalog-controlled fleet records with non-fleet exit classes

verdict.summary: The shell-source interpolation fixes are sound, and the new provider-guard test is non-vacuous for unknown values. However, the guard admits the fleet pseudo-provider for ordinary per-account records even though the code documents fleet as reserved for the zero-dispatch signal. Because workflow input is catalog-controlled, a catalog row can now pass validation as fleet and write semantically invalid fleet health records. This must fail closed before approval.
